title: SEGMENT 14 — The classical pillars: surgery, radiation, chemotherapy

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The classical pillars: surgery, radiation, chemotherapy

I've spent a lot of time on the shiny new approaches — targeted therapy, immunotherapy, genomics — and I want to make sure I don't accidentally give you the impression that the older approaches are obsolete.
They aren't.

The three classical pillars of cancer treatment — surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy — still cure most of the cancers that get cured, and they're often combined with the newer therapies rather than replaced by them.
Let me take each one in turn and tell you, honestly, why it still matters.
Surgery.
For localized cancers, surgery is still the single most curative modality.
A small, contained tumor that hasn't metastasized can often be cut out, and the patient is cured.
Period.
No drug needed.
Most patients cured of cancer are cured by a scalpel, usually combined with some adjuvant — additional — therapy to mop up any residual disease.
Modern surgical oncology is a craft of its own — sentinel lymph node biopsy to determine spread, organ-preserving techniques, robotic assistance, surgery combined with intraoperative imaging.
But the principle is ancient and simple: find the bad tissue, take it out, leave clean margins around it.
The seed-and-soil reasoning works in reverse here — remove the seed before it scatters, and the soil never matters.
Radiation therapy.
Ionizing radiation damages DNA.

If you direct enough of it at cancer cells, you damage them so badly that they die — either through direct catastrophic damage, or because the DNA damage is too much for their (often compromised) repair machinery, or because the damage activates apoptosis.
The trick, and it's a hard one, is hitting the cancer without hitting too much surrounding normal tissue.
Modern radiation oncology has developed beautiful techniques for this.

Fractionation — splitting the total dose into many small daily doses — exploits the fact that normal cells repair sublethal damage between treatments better than cancer cells do, so over many sessions you accumulate more damage in the cancer than in the surroundings.
Conformal techniques like intensity-modulated radiation therapy and proton therapy let you sculpt the radiation dose so that it conforms tightly to the tumor shape and falls off rapidly outside it.
Stereotactic body radiation therapy delivers an enormous dose to a tiny volume in just a few sessions, useful for small isolated tumors.
There's also an unexpected synergy with immunotherapy.
Sometimes, when you irradiate a tumor in one place, tumors elsewhere in the body — that received no radiation — also shrink.
This is called the abscopal effect.
The likely explanation: the irradiated tumor releases its cellular contents and tumor antigens, the immune system gets a clearer look at what to attack, and it goes after distant tumors too.
Combining radiation with checkpoint inhibitors seems to amplify this.
Old physics meets new immunology.
Chemotherapy.
The carpet-bombing campaign.

We talked about why it has the side effects it does — these drugs target cell division broadly, so any tissue that turns over fast (gut lining, bone marrow, hair follicles, sperm and egg cells) takes the hit.
But it still works, and for several cancers — testicular cancer, many leukemias and lymphomas, some pediatric cancers — chemotherapy alone is curative even for advanced disease.
Let me give you the main classes briefly, with one analogy each.
Alkylating agents like cyclophosphamide and the platinums (cisplatin, carboplatin, oxaliplatin) stick chemical groups onto DNA and crosslink the strands.
Imagine glue applied randomly to the pages of a book — pretty soon you can't open it.
Cells with damaged DNA repair pathways are especially vulnerable, which is why platinums work so well in BRCA-mutated cancers.
Antimetabolites like methotrexate, 5-fluorouracil, and gemcitabine sneak into DNA-building pathways disguised as natural building blocks and gum up the synthesis.
Counterfeit lego bricks that lock the machine.
Topoisomerase inhibitors like the anthracyclines (doxorubicin) and irinotecan target the enzymes that manage DNA winding during replication.
The enzymes get trapped mid-cleavage, leaving DNA broken.
Sabotage the machinery that uncoils the rope; the rope tears under tension.
Microtubule poisons like the vinca alkaloids and the taxanes (paclitaxel, docetaxel) interfere with the spindle apparatus that pulls chromosomes apart during mitosis.
The cell tries to divide and gets stuck.
Cut the cables on the elevator while it's going up.
These get combined into regimens with appropriate acronyms — R-CHOP for lymphoma, FOLFIRINOX for pancreatic cancer, ABVD for Hodgkin lymphoma, BEP for testicular cancer.
The combinations are designed so that the toxicities of the individual drugs don't overlap completely and so that the cancer can't easily develop resistance to all of them at once.
The frame to leave with: chemotherapy is crude, but it cures.
The newer therapies are layered on top — sometimes replacing chemo for specific subgroups, often added to it.
The total picture of modern cancer treatment is rarely "instead of chemo"; it's "chemo plus something smarter.
